headlines.title: OpenAI trial live updates: Proceedings resume with more Helen Toner testimony
headlines.description: What to Know Elon Musk has filed a $134 billion lawsuit against Sam Altman and his top lieutenant, Greg Brockman, along with Microsoft over its investments in OpenAI, in 2024. Musk claims OpenAI violated its original mission to operate as a nonprofit focused on developing artificial intelligence for the benefit of humanity. OpenAI has brushed off Musk’s allegations as a case of sour grapes aimed at undercutting its rapid growth and bolstering Musk’s own xAI, which he launched in 2023 as a competitor. The nine-person jury was selected on April 27. The trial is being held in Oakland federal court and scheduled to continue through late May. The OpenAI trial resumes Thursday with more testimony from Helen Toner, who previously served on the OpenAI board. On Wednesday, Canadian VC and former OpenAI board member Shivon Zilis took the stand and was asked about her romantic relationship with Elon Musk. Zilis in her testimony said Musk halted donations to OpenAI after a fight over equity. OpenAI President Greg Brockman took the stand earlier in the week to to discuss how OpenAI was formed and his relationship with Musk, who has filed a $134 billion lawsuit against him and OpenAI CEO Sam Altman, along with Microsoft over its investments in the company. The OpenAI president said he feared Musk would “physically attack” him during a tense confrontation following a disagreement about potential equity shares. The trial centers on the 2015 birth of the ChatGPT maker as a nonprofit startup primarily funded by Musk before evolving into a capitalistic venture now valued at $852 billion. It pits the world’s richest person against Altman, a fellow OpenAI co-founder he accuses of betraying promises to keep the company as a nonprofit dedicated to humanity’s benefit. Musk last week spent three days on the stand before he was excused by the judge. While the Tesla CEO was not in court Monday, he could be recalled to stand again in the trial. NBC Bay Area’s Sergio Quintana is at the federal courthouse in Oakland and providing live updates below.
headlines.source: Sergio Quintana, Kristofer Noceda, Joel Soria and Brendan Weber

headlines.title: 4.2 magnitude earthquake strikes near The Geysers in Sonoma County
headlines.description: A magnitude 4.2 earthquake shook parts of the North Bay and other Northern California areas early Thursday morning, according to the U.S. Geological Survey. The quake struck at 2:42 a.m. Pacific Time near Cobb in Lake County at a depth of about 3 kilometers, or 1.9 miles, the USGS said. The epicenter was about 13 miles north-northeast of Healdsburg, 14 miles southwest of Clearlake and roughly 17 miles north of Windsor, according to seismologists. There were no immediate reports of injuries or damage. BAY AREA QUAKE CENTRAL Bay Area Quake Tracker, the latest earthquake stories, extensive quake prep checklists, videos and many other disaster preparedness resources all in one place: NBCBAYAREA.COM/QUAKES

headlines.title: Fire crews fight a 19-acre fire in the South San Jose hills
headlines.description: A 19-acre brush fire burned in the hills of South San Jose on Wednesday evening. Cal Fire Santa Clara Unit Battalion Chief Steve Clement told NBC Bay Area that firefighters were called about a fire in the area at around 5:40 in the evening. The fire burned near Bernal Road, in the area of Santa Teresa County Park, as well as an IBM facility. Clement said the San Jose Fire Department and Cal Fire battled this fire, along with Cal Fire’s helicopter helping from overhead. He said, despite a strong north wind and some high vegetation in the area, crews were able to contain the fire to 19 acres. Clement explained that fire crews kept the blaze away from nearby homes, but there were downed power lines in the area. “We are unsure if that is the ignition of the fire,” he said. “We have the power line down, and we basically have that area cordoned off until we have that confirmation from PG&E that that power line is de-energized, and then we can get in there and mop up that fire,” he continued. As of 10:20 p.m., Clement confirmed the fire was 100% contained and that crews will be leaving the fire scene for the night, then returning to check on the containment on Thursday. He added that PG&E is working to get those power lines re-energized. No evacuations were issued as a result of this fire, and no injuries were reported, Clement said. More than 2,000 PG&E customers in the area experienced a power outage that evening. According to PG&E’s outage map, power had been restored to all but 15 of those customers by 10:15 p.m. A PG&E spokesperson confirmed to NBC Bay Area that one of the utility’s power lines was down in the area and that once it is safe to approach the power line, crews will be able to secure and fix it. The spokesperson also said that the cause of the outage is still being investigated and that the brush fire did not cause the outage. Clement also noted that this fire was the first large brush fire the Cal Fire SCU unit has responded to so far this year. He urged the public to be cautious and to avoid fire hazards like driving off-road into brush or leaving out cigarette butts. “With the warmer weather, [the vegetation] is starting to turn brown, so we are urging caution if anybody is out in the wildland area,” he said.

headlines.title: Walnut Creek family seeks answers after their loved one was cremated without them knowing
headlines.description: A Walnut Creek family is demanding answers after they said their loved one was cremated without notifying them first. The family said they had one simple request: To be notified before the cremation, so they could say one final goodbye. Now they say that moment was taken away from them. Diana McKean Israel died from stage four ovarian cancer on April 1. Her daughter, Illana Israel Samuels says they asked the Neptune Society in Walnut Creek to notify them before she was cremated. “We were planning on bringing some family members together to be there with her on her next phase of her passing, to be with her when she was cremated,” Samuels said. But the notification never came. And after following up with the Neptune Society this week, Samuels says she was told her mother had already been cremated on April 21. “It was taking something that I see was supposed to be, as I see to be a beautiful and incredible journey of holding her hand when she died and holding her all the way through this process and her illness and all of the thing that are embedded in that, and it was just ripped out from under me,” she said. Samuels says the cremation facility told the family an error was made and has since given them a full refund. But she says no amount of money can replace what they lost. “It almost felt the same way as if someone calls you, out of nowhere, and says someone you loved died,” Samuels said. NBC Bay Area reached out to the Neptune Society in Walnut Creek, asking about the family’s concerns and what steps are being taken to prevent this from happening again. In a statement, they said: “As part of our commitment to all our client families, we guard their privacy and because of this, we do not discuss specific client matters with the media.” When seeing the statement, Samuels said it doesn’t go far enough and still doesn’t answer the family’s biggest question: How will Neptune Society make sure this doesn’t happen to another family? “How about as a part of your commitment and the claim that you America’s most trusted cremation services, that you not make a mistake like this, and tell us how are you going to make sure you don’t make a mistake like this again?,” Samuels said.

headlines.title: Menlo College students frustrated over response to alleged racist commentary
headlines.description: Menlo College is accused of not taking any action to address alleged racist commentary. Two members of the wrestling team said teammates repeatedly used the N word on text threads and in conversations. The students said the administration did little to address the incident after they reported it. The students said they are so frustrated with the racist texts and the administration response, they both plan to transfer to other schools. Even after the wrestling coach held a meeting with the team to discuss the incident, the students report the comments continued. Menlo College’s president issued a statement saying the school is “committed to maintaining a respectful and inclusive community, and takes all reports of misconduct seriously. The college has conducted an investigation into the matter and have taken appropriate steps to address it in accordance with their policies and values.” The college president would not disclose if any students were disciplined, citing privacy rules. The students hope by speaking out about the issue it will help others impacted feel empowered to speak out too.

headlines.title: SF's old Vaillancourt Fountain catches fire as it was being disassembled
headlines.description: A piece of the decades old Vaillancourt Fountain briefly caught fire on Wednesday, as crews resumed disassembling the massive modernist art piece in San Francsico. The smoke was so intense at the start that the nearby Paddle Board Courts had to be temporarily evacuated. The fire was put out quickly and the courts re-opened within minutes. The park rangers say the crew disassembling the fountain was cutting the structure with a blow torch when the rubber tubing and debris inside caught fire. “I think it is ironic that it would catch fire and it just shows you that it is beyond repair. It needs to go,” said Howard Palmer of San Francisco. San Francisco Apr 27 This is the first piece of the 710-ton modern fountain art structure to be removed. Crews first arrived last week to begin disassembling the fountain, but they paused so an appeals court could review an effort by a preservationist group to keep the fountain. However, the court has denied that appeal, so crews returned today to resume jack hammering and weld cutting it apart. The Vaillancourt Fountain has been controversial ever since it was first installed in 1971 and the artist, Armand Vaillancourt, sprayed paint on it. In 1987, the band U2 played a concert in the area, and singer Bono also spray painted on it. He said it was in homage to the sculptor, but Bono was cited and later apologized. The area is so popular with skateboarders that a version of the fountain also exists in Tony Hawk’s Pro Skater videos games. The final decision to take the fountain apart was made after May 2024 when the last pump in the structure failed, the water stopped flowing and the fountain was drained. “The lime scooters, there must have been like 30 or 40 in there and like, it must have been like a treasure hunt to go through it, everything that people lost, a couple bikes,” Benjamin Chang of San Francisco said. Chang works nearby and says it’s bittersweet to see it being taken apart. It will take crews a few weeks to disassemble whole structure. A spokesperson for the SF Arts Commission says the construction crew is updating its disassembly plan to make sure another fire isn’t sparked here. The pieces of the fountain will then go into storage for at least the next three years till the Arts Commission decides what to do with it next.

headlines.title: Taxis, methadone patients, millions of tax dollars at center of Medi-Cal fraud investigation
headlines.description: At least two public agencies in Silicon Valley are investigating alleged fraud involving California’s Medi-Cal program. At issue are millions of tax dollars that have spent transporting patients to a specific drug treatment center in the Bay Area, and allegations some of the drivers responsible for the rides may have been involved in a scheme to inflate how much they were able to charge Medi-Cal for transportation costs. The government probes were spurred by a yearlong NBC Bay Area investigation, which revealed serious concerns regarding Medi-Cal’s taxpayer-funded transportation service for low-income patients traveling to medical appointments. The NBC Bay Area Investigative Unit reviewed hundreds of pages of public records, spent days undercover, and spoke directly with patients to find out how Medi-Cal’s transportation system may be vulnerable to government waste and even abuse. The long line of taxis Nearly every morning, just before sunrise, a steady procession of taxicabs lines up outside an unassuming building in eastern Menlo Park. The facility houses the BAART Programs clinic, a drug treatment center that offers rehabilitative services to those battling opioid addictions, including heroin, fentanyl, and other prescription painkillers. The cabs are there to drop off patients seeking daily doses of methadone, a medication used to reduce cravings and prevent withdrawal symptoms. While the clinic is in San Mateo County, the NBC Bay Area Investigative Unit has learned many of the center’s patients live further south in Santa Clara County and regularly bypass closer treatment locations that Medi-Cal patients are also eligible to use. The taxis are contracted to provide transportation for Medi-Cal patients, but Santa Clara County officials are now investigating allegations some drivers may be routinely offering cash payments to passengers in order to influence them into taking longer car rides to their medical appointments. A whistleblower complaint accuses Silicon Valley taxi drivers of defrauding the government, cheating taxpayers, and exploiting some of California’s most vulnerable — all through a scheme to inflate how much they’re charging taxpayers to transport Medi-Cal patients to their medical appointments. Paid by the mile Taxi drivers charge Medi-Cal by the mile, so the farther they travel, the more money they are paid. A whistleblower complaint alleges some drivers have been inflating what they collect from Medi-Cal by convincing their passengers to travel much farther to receive their drug treatment. According to the complaint, drivers routinely offer cash payments to patients who are willing to pass up other county-approved clinics closer to their homes, and instead undergo drug treatment at the BAART Programs clinic in Menlo Park. For some patients, those trips can stretch as far as 100 miles round trip. Three separate Medi-Cal patients told NBC Bay Area they received cash payments from their taxi drivers, either recently or as far back as five years ago, in exchange for repeatedly requesting them as their official driver for near-daily trips to the BAART Programs clinic in Menlo Park for methadone treatment. NBC Bay Area spoke with three separate BAART Programs patients who said they were paid in cash to repeatedly book the same driver for their trips to the clinic. “I get 10 bucks a day from my driver, but other people get 20,” said one patient, who is unhoused and lives in San Jose. “I’m not sure if every driver does that, or even if they’re allowed to do it.” She said the free rides help her stay in treatment. I get 10 bucks a day from my driver, but other people get 20. A Medi-Cal patient spoke to NBC Bay Area about the cash payments she says she receives from her cab driver each time she receives drug treatment at the BAART Programs Clinic “I’m homeless so getting anywhere is really hard,” she said. “The transportation is making it so that we can actually get here and get our medication.” But when it comes to treatment, there are closer options. Santa Clara County Medi-Cal patients are allowed to use one of four methadone clinics, including the BAART Programs center. The other three clinics, including two in San Jose, are run by the county but don’t get nearly the same volume of government-funded cab rides. The BAART Programs facility in Menlo Park is one of four approved drug treatment centers for Santa Clara County Medi-Cal patients. A lot traffic leads to a lot of questions In the final three months of last year, Medi-Cal paid for more than 22,000 trips to or from the BAART Programs center, according to records from the Santa Clara Family Health Plan, which provides insurance coverage for many of the county’s Medi-Cal patients using a mix of state and federal health care funds. During that same period, trips to the BAART Programs center accounted for 92 percent of all rides to the county’s four approved treatment programs. While there are plenty of legitimate reasons patients may choose a particular medical clinic, the high-volume of long-distance rides to the BAART facility is raising questions. On a single day, NBC Bay Area counted nearly 100 round-trip taxi rides to the clinic. Nearly all were operated by Yellow Checker Cab, which has provided transportation services for Santa Clara County Medi-Cal patients for more than a decade. A single cab ride can cost hundreds Drivers bill Medi-Cal $4 per mile, according to Yellow Checker Cab, so some trips can cost hundreds of dollars. Currently, there are no distance limits on Medi-Cal transportation for methadone treatment. That is now expected to change. As a result of NBC Bay Area’s yearlong investigation and the whistleblower complaint, the health plan said it will soon restrict rides to the nearest clinic that “meets the patient’s needs.” Methadone, which can be administered in either pill or liquid form, helps reduce cravings for recovering drug users as well as lessen withdrawal symptoms. Medi-Cal patients says cab drivers paid them cash for trips to BAART Methadone treatment has long been considered a proven way to stabilize opioid users by reducing cravings and easing withdrawal symptoms. While different factors can influence where patients choose to seek treatment, the volume of rides to the Menlo Park clinic and the payment allegations are caused enough of a concern to gain the attention of government investigators. One patient, who is currently unhoused, told NBC Bay Area he was given $20 a week by his driver, and added the payments were hardly a secret among many of the clinic’s patients. “The way I looked at it is my cab driver was just trying to help me survive, you know what I mean?” he said. “I never really looked at it as an arrangement.” Neither Santa Clara County nor the Santa Clara County Health Plan have released any findings from their investigations, but the probes started with a tip from Juan Garcia, who first contacted NBC Bay Area about his concerns, and later filed a whistleblower complaint directly with the health plan. “It’s always that clinic,” said Garcia. “Why does it have to be that clinic?” Juan Garcia filed a whistleblower complaint with health officials in Santa Clara County, accusing some of defrauding California’s Medi-Cal program. Prior to speaking with government investigators about his concerns, Garcia first shared his story with the NBC Bay Area Investigative Unit. Garcia said two acquaintances told him their cab driver was paying them $20 a day, each, to take longer trips to and from the BAART Programs clinic. “The first thing that came to my mind is like, ‘what the hell?’” Garcia said. It’s always that clinic. Why does it have to be that clinic? Juan Garcia, who filed a whistleblower complaint with government investigators, says the amount of heavy traffic at the clinic from taxpayer-funded cab rides raises serious questions The two passengers regularly travel roughly 50 miles in each direction to get to the clinic, even though they live within just five miles of another approved treatment center. Garcia said he’s even followed them several times, and recorded video of one of those trips. “Wasted, flushed down the toilet, however you want to put it,” Garcia said. “The money’s disappearing and it’s not being used for Medi-Cal.” When contacted by NBC Bay Area, one of the passengers at the center of Garcia’s complaint confirmed getting treatment at BAART Programs, but denied accepting any money from his driver. The other passenger did not respond. The Santa Clara Family Health Plan declined an on-camera interview, but provided a statement saying it takes reports of “health care fraud, waste, or abuse very seriously.” “We are investigating the allegations of improper use of transportation services and are deeply troubled to hear of alleged kickbacks,” the health plan said. “We are working closely with our contracted transportation vendors and will take every possible action to prevent the misuse of taxpayer dollars.” Taxi cab company denies any involvement Larry Silva, president of Yellow Checker Cab, also declined NBC Bay Area’s on-camera interview request, but said his company “maintains a zero-tolerance policy for fraud, waste, or abuse,” and said his business has been unable to verify “any allegations of kickbacks.” Silva said the company’s roughly 200 drivers are classified as independent contractors and are trained on the rules governing Medi-Cal’s transportation benefits. Larry Silva, president of Yellow Checker Cab, says he works with roughly 200 independently contracted taxi drivers between Rainbow, Checker, and Yellow Cab of Silicon Valley. How much money did Medi-Cal pay? The total amount paid to Yellow Checker Cab remains unclear. Silva declined to provide those details. The Santa Clara Family Health Plan also declined to share that information, however, the agency did provide records showing it has paid more than 300,000 claims submitted by Yellow Checker Cab over the past three years for Non-Medical Transportation services (NMT), which are the free rides Medi-Cal patients receive in order to attend medical appointments or pick up prescriptions. About a quarter of all those trips were rides to or from BAART Programs, according to public records. Medi-Cal also pays for a separate classification of transportation services called Non-Emergency Medical Transportation (NEMT) for patients with higher needs, such as those who require a wheelchair van. While Yellow Checker Cab also provides Non-Emergency Medical Transportation services for the health plan, the vast majority of trips for treatment at BAART Programs and other clinics are billed as Non-Medical Transportation. BAART Programs said it is cooperating with the investigations into the alleged payments and said it “does not direct transportation routing decisions.” “BAART Programs does not provide, authorize, direct, or condone financial incentives of any kind for patients or transportation vendors,” a company spokesperson said in a statement. “We are investigating the allegations and partnering with County stakeholders to address the transportation services allegations.” While the Santa Clara Family Health Plan is responsible for approving transportation costs and contracting with cab companies, it’s the county that determines where patients can actually receive treatment and contracts with clinics, such as the BAART Programs center. That relationship stems, in part, from the way Medi-Cal services are split. The Santa Clara Family Health Plan is one of three managed care providers serving Medi-Cal patients in Santa Clara County, but some services — including drug treatment and mental health services — are “carved out” of what the plans cover directly and are instead handled by the county. A spokesperson for Santa Clara County said it’s conducting a “thorough investigation” of the alleged driver payments, and said it follows “all applicable Medi-Cal requirements and remains committed to ensuring that patients understand their options and can access the life-saving treatment they need.” The county said it’s required to make services available close to where people live, but that “patients always have the right to choose where they receive treatment.” While the Santa Clara Family Health Plan typically provides the “lowest cost” medical transportation to the “closest provider” that meets a patient’s needs, those restrictions have not applied to carved-out services such as methadone treatment. The health plan, however, says it now plans to enact similar travel restrictions for methadone treatment in the near future. To ensure that our members have convenient access to medically necessary substance use treatment services without pressure to seek care elsewhere, Santa Clara Family Health Plan will soon begin limiting transportation services to the closest location that will meet their needs. A spokesperson for the Santa Clara Family Health Plan “To ensure that our members have convenient access to medically necessary substance use treatment services without pressure to seek care elsewhere, Santa Clara Family Health Plan will soon begin limiting transportation services to the closest location that will meet their needs,” the health plan said in a statement. To determine how much those taxi rides to BAART Programs have been costing taxpayers, NBC Bay Area logged how far cabs were traveling to drop off passengers once they left the clinic. Over the course of 20 rides, the cabs averaged close to 30 miles in one direction, meaning the trip to the clinic and back likely cost roughly $240. A quarter of those trips likely cost about $400 roundtrip, with riders traveling all the way to Gilroy – about 50 miles in a single direction. When asked why they were willing to travel so far to get treatment at BAART Programs when there were closer options operated by the county, many patients simply said they preferred the Menlo Park clinic because it tends to be more forgiving when patients relapse or fail mandatory drug tests, which might get them kicked out of other programs. “So, if you fail a drug test because you’re testing positive for meth or something else, they’re not going to cut your dose or anything crazy like that,” one patient said. Another patient, who did not want to be identified, agreed, saying BAART Programs was more empathetic, and said kicking patients out of recovery can lead them back to drugs such as fentanyl, sometimes with deadly consequences. “I’ve lost so many people…you have no idea,” the patient said. “People I love and care about, who I’ll never see again.” In a statement, a spokesperson for BAART Programs said it uses an “evidence-based model of care” to reduce overdose risk and complies with all federal and state requirements. The BAART Programs center in Menlo Park said it is now investigating allegations that taxi drivers have been offering cash payments to Medi-Cal patients as a way to recruit them to receive their drug treatment at the BAART clinic. According to a whistleblower complaint, cab drivers are offering the payments in an effort to boost their own Medi-Cal mileage rates and paychecks by specifically focusing on individuals who live farther away from the clinic. Several health experts who spoke to NBC Bay Area said the high volume of cab rides to BAART Programs raised red flags and warranted investigation, while others said putting too many restrictions in place might discourage some patients from seeking treatment, which could lead to unintended consequences, including higher costs in the future. “When we’re thinking about putting up hurdles to people accessing care, we want to do the math to make sure that we’re not actually increasing the likelihood that people are going to need to use the hospital later on,” said Amanda Brewster, a UC Berkeley public health professor who studies how health systems use non-medical services to improve outcomes for patients. “You end up with that pool of money having to pay for really expensive services.” While government audits have repeatedly raised concerns about Medi-Cal fraud, Brewster said adequate oversight can be costly. “It costs money to follow the money,” she said, adding that health plans are trying to maximize the amount of money they spend on healthcare. Amanda Brewster is co-Director of the Center for Health Management and Policy Research at the UC Berkeley School of Public Health. Her research explores how non-medical services, like free transportation, can improve outcomes for patients. It’s unclear where the investigations by Santa Clara County and Santa Clara Family Health Plan will lead, but it’s likely the findings will be sent to the California Department of Health Care Services. Once an investigation into fraud, waste, or abuse is complete, the health plan has ten business days to submit its findings to the state for potential follow-up, which can include possible criminal action. The California Department of Health Care Services told NBC Bay Area it is aware of the allegations but does not comment on ongoing investigations. With Medi-Cal already facing a potential multibillion-dollar shortfall, Garcia said he hopes the investigation leads to measurable reform that can actually stop individuals from siphoning off money from Medi-Cal. “They’re taking what they can,” he said. “Somebody needs to put a stop to it.” Contact The Investigative Unit submit tips | 1-888-996-TIPS | e-mail Bigad Follow @BigadShaban on social media Bigad Shaban
headlines.source: Bigad Shaban, Michael Bott, Jeremy Carroll, Michael Horn, Anasooya Thorakkattu and Anjana Dasam

headlines.title: Public weighs in on proposed gas water heater ban
headlines.description: New rules that aim to eventually transition natural gas water heaters to newer technology brought a packed house to San Francisco Wednesday. The Bay Area Air District discussed potential flexibility and affordability options for some residents. “Air district staff are presenting to the board of directors on potential exemptions about the appliance rules,” said Miranda Iglesias with the air district. “These rules are really important for the public health and for clean air in the Bay Area.” Under rules approved in 2023, they would apply when a gas water heater or furnace is being replaced or fails and requires replacement after a compliance date. In 2027, only zero-nitrogen oxide water heaters can be sold or installed – costing about $3,500 more than a traditional heater. Existing water heaters can remain until they burn out. More than 100 people signed up to comment Wednesday. A decision is expected in the fall. NBC Bay Area’s Christie Smith has more in the video report above.

headlines.title: A closer look at how tourism shapes the future of Oakland
headlines.description: City leaders, businesses and community members got a snapshot of how visitors are helping shape the town’s future on Wednesday’s Impact 510: The Town Tourism Day at the Oakland Museum of California. Numbers released Wednesday show in 2025, 3.2 million visitors created a total economic impact of $740 million and helped support more than 5,200 jobs. “Wherever I go, people are happy about helping their city move forward, and I see people moving forward and it’s not just the mayor or city councilmembers, this is our entire city moving forward,” said city Mayor Barbara Lee. “Being named the number one food city in America two years in a row brings resurgence to our restaurants, brings our restaurant and culinary alive. Events like Super Bowl and even NBA All-Star from last year is bringing sports into the forefront,” said Peter Gamez, Visit Oakland CEO. The numbers are slightly lower than the year before, but day visitations are up. With major sports events including the Austrian soccer team staying in Oakland, leading up to the FIFA World Cup and global moments, Visit Oakland expects those tourism numbers to rise. “When Alysa Liu received her gold medal, we saw an increase in request for Oakland, what is going on in Oakland,” Gamez said. “Then with Ryan Coogler and Khelani with a Grammy, all these golden moments are putting Oakland back in the center in people’s minds”. That positivity is helping drive new business. Entrepreneurs behind Hysteria Sports Bar, set to become Oakland’s first women’s sports bar and also expected to open in 2027 in time for the women’s World Cup, say the timing is right. “It’s really encouraging to see the numbers, particularly because we are opening a business in an industry that is known to be one of the hardest,” said Erin Waddell, co-founder of Hysteria Sports Bar. “We are so excited we are looking to be located here in Oakland because the town needs it, the town deserves it and the town is waiting on it,” said Audacious Wilson, co-founder of Hysteria Sports Bar. The report also highlights challenges with international travel down nationwide. But Oakland is seeing a bright spot. Travel from Mexico is up 6%. Lee says overall momentum can be felt, pointing to safe, successful events and a community pushing forward together. “We have our challenges we have to understand and move forward with and deal with, but I think looking at the opportunities and the upside of what is going on, Oakland is definitely on the move,” Lee said.

headlines.title: Stretch of I-80 in San Francisco named in honor of Giants legend Willie Mays
headlines.description: In celebration of what would have been Willie Mays’ 95th birthday, Caltrans has named a stretch of Interstate 80 in San Francisco in honor of the baseball legend. The portion of I-80 near Oracle Park now features the name Willie Mays Highway. “Willie’s talent, joy, and connection to the San Francisco Giants shaped a baseball culture that still defines the Bay Area,” Caltrans said in a statement. “Caltrans is honored to highlight his story on our highways and recognize a legend who has meant so much to generations of Bay Area fans.” Yesterday, Caltrans installed new highway signage for the Willie Mays Highway Memorial on westbound I-80 in SF, celebrating his birthday and legacy. Willie’s talent, joy, and connection to the San Francisco Giants shaped a baseball culture that still defines the Bay Area. pic.twitter.com/OUJpwO2WFt — Caltrans District 4 (@CaltransD4) May 6, 2026

headlines.title: Man charged with murder in San Francisco hit and run drops defense attorney
headlines.description: A man facing a murder charge over a hit and run in San Francisco last month has decided to drop his defense attorney and seek new counsel. Valentino Amil, 30, has been charged by the San Francisco District Attorney’s Office with murder and felony leaving the scene of an accident after he allegedly intentionally ran over a woman with his car in the afternoon of April 13. Amil, who was driving a car with his wife and two children inside, was pulling out of a gas station in the area of Mission Street and South Van Ness Avenue and blocking the sidewalk. Dannielle Spillman, 74, walked up to the car and a brief argument occurred, according to the district attorney’s office. Spillman then walked in front of the car and apparently emptied liquid from a water bottle onto the hood of the car. That’s when Amil allegedly accelerated, striking Spillman, who ended up on the hood of the car. Spillman then fell into the roadway as the car allegedly ran her over and then fled the scene, according to District Attorney Brooke Jenkins. Amil appeared in court Wednesday for what was scheduled to be his preliminary hearing. Instead, Amil’s defense attorney Seth Morris told a judge that Amil and his family requested to drop Morris from the case. “Mr. Amil and his family are asking that I be relieved of counsel,” Morris said to San Francisco Superior Court Judge Brian Stretch. San Francisco Apr 16 San Francisco Apr 15 Morris had previously argued that Amil’s alleged actions were self-defense, saying that Amil reacted out of fear and was trying to protect his family. Zhivago Redon, Amil’s father, also showed up to court Wednesday. In an interview, Redon did not disclose the reasons for deciding to seek a new defense attorney. However, he did express what he sees as unfair treatment of Amil from the district attorney’s office, saying that what occurred does not amount to a murder charge, which falls under California Penal Code 187. “This has been turned into a hate train by the district attorney’s office,” Redon said. “I would like to know if Ms. Jenkins had read the discovery before she signed off on a 187 charge on my son.” Attorney Robert Waggener showed up to court Wednesday as a possible new choice for Amil’s defense attorney. “The family has asked me to potentially take on the case,” Waggener said in an interview. Waggener is expected to appear in court again May 15, when a judge will formally confirm who will be Amil’s new representation. Outside the courtroom, Redon tried to paint Amil as a harmless family man. “He’s never been in trouble in his life,” Redon said. “He stands for community, he stands for love, he stands for peace and there is nothing that’s embedded in his spirit or soul of any kind of charge that was applied and insinuated.” Amil waived his right to have a preliminary hearing within 10 days of arraignment. A 60-day extension was given, with the possibility of the hearing being scheduled for June 23. “Valentino is emotionally, physically, not doing well,” Redon said. “He’s hurt emotionally for the person that passed. He’s hurt for his family.”

headlines.title: Man found guilty of attempted murder in 2022 shooting of Mountain View cop
headlines.description: A jury on Wednesday found a man guilty of attempted murder in the 2022 close-range shooting of a Mountain View police officer, according to the Santa Clara County District Attorney’s Office. Jeffrey Choy, 37, of Union City shot the officer from an arm’s length away during a traffic stop on July 16, 2022, the district attorney’s office said. The officer was injured but has since recovered and returned to duty. Choy faces life in prison, according to the district attorney’s office. Sentencing is expected by late summer or early fall. “This verdict reminds us that every time a law enforcement officer starts their shift, they are risking their lives to protect us and our families,” District Attorney Jeff Rosen said in a statement. “I thank this officer and every officer who has made it their professional responsibility to put themselves between our community and extremely dangerous people.” The officer had been conducting a DUI enforcement shift in downtown Mountain View when he stopped a driver that he saw run a stop sign near Villa and Hope streets, the district attorney’s office said. The officer walked up to the driver’s door and the two exchanged greetings, according to the district attorney’s office. Choy then pulled out a handgun and fired twice. One bullet missed, but the other bullet went through the officer’s arm and struck his bullet-resistant vest. Choy drove off but crashed his car near Higdon Avenue, the district attorney’s office said. He fled on foot and hid in a laundry room for hours before escaping the city. He was arrested the next day outside a Fremont diner where he was having brunch with his family, according to the district attorney’s office. “This case and this finding is about justice for our officer,” Mountain View police Chief Michael Canfield said. “It is about healing for our officer, our department and our community.” At the time of the shooting, Choy was a felon who was prohibited from having any weapons, the district attorney’s office said. He was out on bail and had an active warrant for possessing a gun that was found during a traffic stop in March 2022. Prosecutor Marisa McKeown said she was satisfied with the verdict but frustrated it took about four years to reach a conclusion. She said she fully expects Choy to receive a life sentence. “This is a repeat offender,” McKeown said. “He’s a three striker. He was out on bail, and he is not allowed to even have a firearm, let alone shoot a police officer with it. He’s facing life in prison, not the least of which because he shot a police officer but also because of his lengthy life of crime.” The officer was in the courtroom Wednesday, but he was not identified by the department.
